title: Procgen Tool Selection Decision Tree
summary: Decision tree for picking the right tool from Mosaic's 17 procgen/* tools given a user intent. Wrong-tool-for-the-job is the single largest procgen failure mode: LLMs default to procgen/noise-generate for point scatter (clusters — wrong; use procgen/poisson-disk), reach for procgen/wfc for caves (needs an authored tileset; procgen/cellular-automata is the native answer), or call procgen/marching-cubes without a density field. This entry provides intent-to-tool mapping, brief algorithm primers, canonical pipelines, failure modes, and a short decision tree. Read before choosing any procgen tool when the user's intent is not already bound to a specific algorithm name.

llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_a_natural_looking_hei: Use procgen/noise-generate (fBm Perlin/Simplex/Ridged). Feed into procgen/terrain or terrain/height. Do NOT use poisson-disk, voronoi, or cellular-automata for continuous heights — their outputs are discrete.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_to_scatter_trees_rocks_or: Use procgen/poisson-disk (guarantees minimum spacing) or procgen/poisson-sample. Never use procgen/noise-generate for this — noise clusters and leaves gaps, the opposite of even scatter.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_clustered_distributio: Threshold procgen/noise-generate above a cutoff and sample, or use procgen/blue-noise with negative bias. Blue noise without bias gives even spread, not clusters.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_organic_caves_or_cell: Use procgen/cellular-automata with Game-of-Life-like rules (B678/S345678 is the classic cave rule). Do NOT use procgen/wfc — it needs an authored tileset and will produce contradictions without one.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_a_tile_based_layout_w: Use procgen/wfc. Confirm the user has (or can supply) a tileset with adjacency constraints; see kb://procgen/wfc-tileset-authoring. Without a tileset, recommend cellular-automata (organic) or noise (continuous) instead.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_a_plant_tree_or_branc: Use procgen/lsystem with a grammar (axiom + rewrite rules + turtle interpretation). For game-quality vegetation with wind/LOD, recommend Unity's SpeedTree instead — L-systems produce generic branching geometry, not shaded foliage.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_biome_regions_crystal: Use procgen/voronoi (partitions space by nearest seed). For road networks or connected paths use the DUAL graph from procgen/delaunay — voronoi gives regions, delaunay gives connectivity.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_a_mesh_from_a_volumet: Pipeline: procgen/sdf-generate builds the signed distance field, procgen/marching-cubes extracts the isosurface mesh. Never call marching-cubes without a density field input — it needs a 3D scalar grid.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_to_make_existing_terrain_: Use procgen/hydraulic-erode on the existing heightmap. Input is a heightmap; output is an eroded heightmap. Do NOT regenerate with noise — erosion carves channels and ridges that noise cannot express.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_a_whole_planet_or_sph: Use procgen/planet. Do NOT try to wrap procgen/noise-generate around a sphere manually — planet handles cubesphere projection, seam stitching, and pole singularities.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_spots_stripes_or_anim: Use procgen/reaction-diffusion (Gray-Scott). Parameter regimes give spots, stripes, mazes, or mitosis-like patterns. Noise cannot produce the characteristic connectivity of Turing patterns.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_for_a_volumetric_fog_clou: Use procgen/noise-3d-texture. The 2D procgen/noise-generate is not a drop-in for volumetrics — a 3D field requires 3D noise sampling to avoid stacking-artifact layers.
llmGuidance.when_intent_is_ambiguous: Ask: (a) is the output points, grid, or mesh? (b) do you need gaps between outputs or are clusters OK? (c) do you have authored rules/tiles or should the system invent them? Answers collapse the decision tree to one tool in almost every case.

volumetricDensityCloud.notes: 3D noise volume for fog, clouds, ore density.

evenPointDistribution.notes: Bridson algorithm; guarantees minimum radius r between samples.

sparsePointSampling.notes: Sampling variant — draws N points with Poisson-disk property.

blueNoiseTexture.notes: Void-and-cluster; high-frequency energy, no low-frequency clumps.

organicCaveTopology.notes: Game-of-Life-style iteration; classic B678/S345678 cave rule.

parametricCaveWorkflow.notes: Opinionated cave generator; wraps CA with post-processing.

tileBasedConstrainedLayout.notes: Wave Function Collapse; requires authored tileset + adjacency rules.

plantOrBranchingGrammar.notes: Rewrite rules → turtle interpretation → line/mesh geometry.

regionPartitioning.notes: Biome maps, crystal cells, cracked surfaces. Partitions by nearest seed.

sparseTriangulation.notes: Dual graph of Voronoi. Use for connectivity between seeds (roads, wire networks).

implicitSurface.notes: Build a signed distance field from primitives + Boolean ops.

volumetricMeshExtraction.notes: Extracts isosurface mesh from a 3D scalar field at a threshold.

terrainErosion.notes: Particle-based water simulation on an existing heightmap.

sphericalWorld.notes: Full planet generator; cubesphere + noise + biomes.

turingPattern.notes: Gray-Scott 2-chemical simulation; produces spots, stripes, mazes.

heightmapToTerrain.notes: Convenience wrapper: noise → Unity Terrain heightmap.

algorithmPrimers.perlinSimplexNoise: Gradient noise: smooth interpolation between random gradient vectors at lattice points. Produces continuous, differentiable, naturally-correlated values. Stacking octaves (fractal Brownian motion) adds detail; ridged variants absolute-value the signal for mountain-like ridges.
algorithmPrimers.poissonDiskSampling: Distributes points so every pair is at least radius r apart. Bridson's algorithm uses an active-sample queue and a spatial grid for O(n) performance. The guarantee of minimum spacing is what distinguishes it from random or noise sampling.
algorithmPrimers.blueNoise: A spectral characterization: energy concentrated at high frequencies, no low-frequency clumps. Perceptually even without the regularity of a grid. Void-and-cluster is the canonical offline construction.
algorithmPrimers.cellularAutomata: A grid of cells with a transition rule applied each step. For cave generation a neighborhood count rule (e.g., become wall if >=5 neighbors are walls) run for ~5 iterations on random noise produces connected organic caverns.
algorithmPrimers.waveFunctionCollapse: Tile placement under adjacency constraints. Each cell holds a superposition of possible tiles; collapse the lowest-entropy cell, propagate constraints, repeat. Without an authored tileset there is nothing to collapse.
algorithmPrimers.lSystem: A formal grammar: an axiom string and rewrite rules expanded N times, then interpreted by a turtle (F=forward, +/- turn, [/] push/pop) to draw geometry.
algorithmPrimers.voronoi: Given seed points, every spatial cell is assigned to its nearest seed. Produces a polygonal partition of space. Natural for biomes, territories, crystal structures.
algorithmPrimers.delaunayTriangulation: The unique triangulation where no point lies inside any triangle's circumscribed circle. It is the dual graph of the Voronoi diagram: connecting seeds across shared Voronoi edges.
algorithmPrimers.signedDistanceField: A scalar field where each value is the signed distance to the nearest surface — negative inside, zero on surface, positive outside. Supports clean Boolean operations (min/max/subtract) and smooth blends.
algorithmPrimers.marchingCubes: Extracts a polygonal mesh from a 3D scalar field at a chosen isovalue. For each voxel, looks up one of 256 triangulation cases based on which corners are above/below the threshold.
algorithmPrimers.reactionDiffusion: Two chemicals diffuse and react via Gray-Scott kinetics. Parameter pairs (feed rate F, kill rate k) select regimes: spots, stripes, holes, mitosis. The classic source of Turing patterns.
algorithmPrimers.hydraulicErosion: Spawns water droplets, accumulates sediment capacity proportional to slope and velocity, deposits/erodes as the droplet descends. Iterated thousands of times, it carves rivers, valleys, and ridges.

canonicalPipelines.name: Procedural Terrain
canonicalPipelines.notes: Noise alone reads as 'video-game'; add erosion to get valleys and ridges. See kb-terrain-system-primer.

canonicalPipelines.name: Prefab Scatter
canonicalPipelines.notes: Never use noise for positions — clusters + gaps look wrong. Use noise only as a density MASK over the Poisson points.

canonicalPipelines.name: Organic Mesh from Implicit
canonicalPipelines.notes: SDF is the input; marching-cubes is the extractor. Cannot swap order.

canonicalPipelines.name: Structured Cave
steps: procgen/wfc seeded by the CA output (tile detail)
canonicalPipelines.notes: CA gives cavern shape; WFC fills detail with authored tiles. Requires a tileset.

canonicalPipelines.name: Biome Map
canonicalPipelines.notes: Voronoi for discrete biomes, noise for continuous within-biome variation.

canonicalPipelines.name: Road Network
canonicalPipelines.notes: Voronoi gives regions, NOT connectivity. Delaunay is the right dual for roads/paths.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Trees or rocks end up in dense patches with large empty gaps.
commonPitfalls.cause: Used procgen/noise-generate as a scatter source. Noise is continuous and clusters — it cannot guarantee spacing.
commonPitfalls.fix: Switch to procgen/poisson-disk (min-radius guarantee) or threshold-sample procgen/blue-noise (even high-frequency distribution).

commonPitfalls.symptom: procgen/wfc returns contradictions or runs without producing output.
commonPitfalls.cause: Called without a tileset or with incomplete adjacency rules; the solver finds no consistent assignment.
commonPitfalls.fix: Author a tileset first (see kb://procgen/wfc-tileset-authoring). If the goal was organic caves, use procgen/cellular-automata instead — no tileset required.

commonPitfalls.symptom: procgen/marching-cubes returns empty or errors on missing input.
commonPitfalls.cause: Called without a 3D scalar density field. Marching cubes is an extractor, not a generator.
commonPitfalls.fix: Build the field first with procgen/sdf-generate or procgen/noise-3d-texture, then pass it to marching-cubes.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Generated a Voronoi diagram expecting a road network; got disconnected polygons.
commonPitfalls.cause: Voronoi outputs regions, not paths. The edges between cells are boundaries, not routes.
commonPitfalls.fix: Use procgen/delaunay (the dual graph) — its edges connect seed points and form a natural path/road skeleton.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Tried to generate a spherical world by projecting 2D noise onto a sphere; got pinching at poles and seams at the equator.
commonPitfalls.cause: UV wrapping of 2D noise does not respect spherical topology.
commonPitfalls.fix: Use procgen/planet which handles cubesphere projection and domain stitching, OR sample 3D noise on the sphere surface.

commonPitfalls.symptom: L-system tree looks geometric and lifeless; wind / LOD / material are missing.
commonPitfalls.cause: L-systems produce generic branching geometry only. They are not a vegetation pipeline.
commonPitfalls.fix: For game-quality trees, recommend Unity SpeedTree Modeler + SpeedTree runtime. Use procgen/lsystem for organic-growth patterns (coral, roots, lightning) where SpeedTree doesn't apply.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Cellular-automata cave has floating islands or unreachable chambers.
commonPitfalls.fix: Post-process: flood-fill label components, keep the largest, optionally cut corridors to merge others. Not a tool-selection error — a post-processing step.

decisionRules.rule: procgen/noise-generate with fBm octaves; downstream procgen/terrain.

decisionRules.scenario: Intent is 'distribute N objects with guaranteed spacing'
decisionRules.evidence: Bridson 2007 SIGGRAPH sketch on Poisson-disk sampling.

decisionRules.rule: procgen/cellular-automata with cave rule (B678/S345678). Not procgen/wfc (needs tileset).

decisionRules.scenario: Intent is 'tile-based layout with authored constraints'
decisionRules.rule: procgen/wfc. Verify tileset exists first — see kb://procgen/wfc-tileset-authoring.

decisionRules.scenario: Output is a mesh from a volume or implicit
Input is an existing heightmap; user wants more realism

decisionRules.rule: Classify output type first (points / grid / mesh), then whether the user has authored rules/tiles or expects the system to invent. That collapses the tree to one tool.
decisionRules.evidence: Empirical: 90% of wrong-tool picks are resolved by answering these two questions.

authorshipNotes: Content authored by the Mosaic team as original editorial prose. Algorithm names (Perlin, Simplex, Poisson-disk, Voronoi, Delaunay, Wave Function Collapse, Marching Cubes, Gray-Scott, Bridson, L-system) and their mathematical definitions are facts from publicly-published procgen literature and are not copyrightable. All decision rules, intent-to-tool mappings, canonical pipelines, pitfalls, and the decision-tree prose are original Mosaic editorial content. Cited papers (Perlin 1985, Bridson 2007, Lorensen & Cline 1987, Turing 1952, Prusinkiewicz & Lindenmayer — The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants is freely available online, Gumin 2016 is MIT-licensed) are referenced for authorship provenance; no prose is reproduced from them.
